era_true.statement: Athens is located in the region called Attica.
The Spartans defeated Athens in the Peloponnesian War.
The city of Thebes lies in Boeotia.
Marathon is a plain northeast of Athens.
The Persians invaded Greece under King Xerxes.
The battle of Salamis was a naval engagement fought in a narrow strait.
Pericles led Athens during a period of great building and expansion.
The Parthenon stands on the Acropolis of Athens.
Socrates was put on trial in Athens on charges of impiety and corrupting the youth.
The Athenian assembly meets on the Pnyx hill.
Athenian citizens vote by show of hands in the assembly.
Ostracism allows Athenians to exile a citizen for ten years by popular vote.
The archon basileus oversees religious cases in Athens.
The Council of Five Hundred prepares business for the Athenian assembly.
Jurors in Athenian courts are selected by lot from the citizen body.
Cleisthenes reorganized the Athenian tribes into ten new tribes.
Solon reformed the laws of Athens and abolished debt slavery.
The tyrant Peisistratos seized power in Athens three times.
Draco established the first written laws in Athens.
Miltiades commanded the Athenian forces at the battle of Marathon.
Themistocles persuaded Athens to build a large fleet of triremes.
The Spartans held the pass at Thermopylae against the Persian army.
Leonidas was the Spartan king who died at Thermopylae.
The Delian League was originally headquartered on the island of Delos.
Athens moved the treasury of the Delian League from Delos to Athens.
The Long Walls connected Athens to its port at Piraeus.
A plague struck Athens early in the Peloponnesian War and killed many citizens.
Pericles died during the plague that struck Athens.
Cleon was an Athenian demagogue who advocated harsh treatment of Mytilene.
The Sicilian Expedition ended in the total destruction of the Athenian force.
Alcibiades defected from Athens to Sparta during the Sicilian campaign.
The Thirty Tyrants ruled Athens briefly after the Peloponnesian War ended.
Thrasybulus led the democratic restoration in Athens after the rule of the Thirty.
Sparta maintains a system with two kings ruling simultaneously.
Spartan boys enter military training at the age of seven.
Helots work the land in Laconia and Messenia for the Spartans.
The Corinthians are skilled shipbuilders and traders.
Corinth controls the narrow isthmus connecting the Peloponnese to central Greece.
The Oracle at Delphi speaks prophecies through a priestess called the Pythia.
The sanctuary at Olympia hosts athletic games every four years.
Zeus is the king of the gods and rules from Mount Olympus.
Athena is the patron goddess of Athens.
Poseidon is the god of the sea and earthquakes.
Apollo is the god of prophecy, music, and the sun.
Hermes serves as the messenger of the gods.
Hades rules the underworld where the dead reside.
Prometheus stole fire from the gods and gave it to mortals.
Heracles performed twelve labors as penance.
The Trojan War was fought over Helen, who was taken to Troy.
Odysseus wandered for ten years after the fall of Troy before reaching Ithaca.
Achilles was the greatest warrior among the Greeks at Troy.
Theseus slew the Minotaur in the labyrinth on Crete.
The Argonauts sailed with Jason to retrieve the Golden Fleece.
Oedipus solved the riddle of the Sphinx at Thebes.
The Amazons were a race of warrior women.
Hoplites fight in a close formation called the phalanx.
A trireme is a warship powered by three banks of oars.
Bronze is used to make armor, weapons, and tools.
Iron is harder than bronze and is used for weapons and tools.
The Nile River floods its banks every year and makes the surrounding land fertile.
Egypt is ruled by kings called pharaohs.
The Egyptians build enormous stone pyramids as tombs for their kings.
The Phoenicians invented an alphabet of individual letter signs.
Carthage is a Phoenician colony on the coast of North Africa.
The Phoenician city of Tyre is located on the coast of the eastern sea.
Babylon lies between the Tigris and Euphrates rivers.
The Persians conquered the Lydian kingdom and captured King Croesus.
Cyrus the Great founded the Persian Empire.
Darius organized the Persian Empire into provinces called satrapies.
The Royal Road runs from Sardis to Susa across the Persian Empire.
The Scythians are nomadic horsemen living north of the Black Sea.
The Black Sea lies north of Anatolia.
The Hellespont is a narrow strait separating Europe from Asia.
Sicily is the largest island in the western Mediterranean Sea.
Syracuse is the most powerful Greek city in Sicily.
The Pillars of Heracles mark the western boundary of the inner sea.
Massalia is a Greek colony in the land of the Gauls.
Cyrene is a Greek colony on the coast of North Africa.
The Danube is a large river flowing into the Black Sea.
Crete is a large island south of the Aegean Sea.
The island of Rhodes lies off the southwestern coast of Anatolia.
Tin must be combined with copper to produce bronze.
Gold is a metal that does not rust or tarnish.
Silver is mined at Laurion in Attica.
Olive trees grow throughout Attica and produce oil used for food, light, and cleaning.
Grapes are pressed to make wine.
Wheat and barley are the principal grain crops.
The earth is surrounded by water according to the shape of a sphere.
The morning star and the evening star are the same celestial body.
The moon reflects light from the sun rather than producing its own light.
Eclipses of the moon occur when the earth's shadow falls upon it.
The sun travels across the sky from east to west each day.
The stars appear to rotate around a fixed point in the northern sky each night.
There are five wandering stars visible to the eye that move against the fixed stars.
A right triangle's square on the longest side equals the sum of the squares on the other two sides.
The diagonal of a square is not commensurable with its side.
A circle can be divided into three hundred and sixty degrees.
Magnets attract iron.
Amber rubbed with wool attracts light objects like chaff.
Sound travels more slowly than light, as thunder follows lightning.
Hot air rises above cold air.
Water flows downhill and seeks the lowest available level.
Evaporated seawater leaves salt behind.
Fresh water can be obtained by boiling and condensing seawater.
Earthquakes are common in the Greek lands and on the islands.
Volcanoes exist on the island of Sicily, including Mount Etna which erupts fire and rock.
Rivers carry silt to the sea and build up new land at their mouths.
Fossils of sea creatures are found in rocks far from the sea.
Herodotus wrote an account of the wars between the Greeks and the Persians.
Thucydides wrote a history of the war between Athens and Sparta.
Aeschylus, Sophocles, and Euripides wrote tragedies performed in Athens.
Aristophanes wrote comedies performed in Athens.
Hippocrates of Cos practiced medicine based on observation rather than divine causation.
Pythagoras taught that numbers underlie the structure of all things.
Anaxagoras taught that the sun is a mass of burning stone larger than the Peloponnese.
Democritus proposed that all matter is composed of tiny indivisible particles called atoms.
Empedocles taught that all matter is composed of four roots: earth, water, air, and fire.
Heraclitus taught that fire is the fundamental element and all things are in constant flux.
Parmenides argued that change is an illusion and true reality is unchanging.
Zeno of Elea devised paradoxes to show that motion is logically impossible.

era_false.statement: Athens is located on the island of Crete.
The Persian Wars were fought against the Egyptians.
Sparta was a coastal island city with no land army.
The Battle of Marathon was fought at sea.
Pericles was a king of Sparta.
The Nile River flows through the center of Athens.
Homer composed the Iliad in Latin.
The Olympic Games originated in Persia.
Socrates was a famous Spartan general who conquered Athens.
The Parthenon was built to honor the god Ares.
Greece is a landlocked country with no access to the sea.
Xerxes was the elected leader of the Athenian democracy.
The Peloponnesian War was fought between Athens and Egypt.
Herodotus wrote exclusively about Roman history.
Themistocles commanded the Persian fleet at Salamis.
The Acropolis sits in a low valley below the city of Athens.
Olives cannot grow in the soil of Attica.
The sun rises in the west over the Aegean Sea.
Darius invaded Greece from the southern coast of Africa.
The Athenian Assembly was restricted to women only.
Corinth is located north of Thrace.
Bronze is softer than unbaked clay.
Wheat grows underwater in the rivers of Greece.
The Spartans were famous for their love of luxury and long speeches.
Thermopylae is a wide open plain with no narrow pass.
Thucydides wrote a history of the Trojan War.
Wine is made by fermenting stones.
The Greeks learned writing from the Spartans, who invented the alphabet.
Horses have six legs.
Dolphins are a type of bird found in Greek forests.
Ships sink when placed on calm water.
Gold is lighter than feathers of equal size.
The Greek language uses Egyptian hieroglyphs for writing.
Athens had no navy during the Persian Wars.
Fire is cold to the touch and freezes water.
The Aegean Sea is a freshwater lake.
Olive oil is extracted from pine cones.
Solon was a tyrant of Persia who never visited Athens.
The moon produces its own light brighter than the sun.
Tragedy was invented in Sparta as a military exercise.
Aeschylus wrote comedies and never composed a tragedy.
The agora of Athens was located on top of Mount Olympus.
Iron is softer than wool.
Humans can breathe underwater without any aid.
Sheep produce silk and silkworms produce wool.
The Hellespont connects the Aegean Sea to the western ocean.
Cleisthenes established a monarchy in Athens.
The Iliad tells the story of Odysseus returning home from Troy.
Pythagoras claimed the earth is shaped like a cube.
Marble is a soft material that cannot be carved.
The Battle of Salamis was a land battle fought in Egypt.
Draco's laws were famous for their extreme leniency.
Athens and Sparta were the same city with one government.
Snow is warm and falls only in summer in Greece.
Grapes are harvested in winter from beneath the snow.
The Odyssey describes a journey that lasted only one day.
Trireme warships were powered by sails alone and had no oars.
Copper and tin combine to make pure gold.
Miltiades led the Persian army against Greece at Marathon.
The Delian League was led by Sparta against Athens.
Hesiod wrote that the gods live beneath the sea.
Phoenicians learned their alphabet from the Athenians.
The Mediterranean Sea freezes solid every winter.
Hippocrates taught that disease is caused only by angry trees.
Pindar was a famous Athenian potter who never wrote poetry.
Leather is made from fish scales in Greece.
The archon was a hereditary position passed from father to son in democratic Athens.
Euripides was a Spartan admiral who never wrote plays.
Clay becomes softer when fired in a kiln.
Sappho was a famous male wrestler from Corinth.
The Tigris and Euphrates rivers flow through the Peloponnese.
Mount Olympus is the lowest point in all of Greece.
Aristophanes wrote only funeral hymns and never composed comedy.
Grain can grow without water or soil.
The Persians spoke Greek as their native language.
Salamis is an inland mountain far from any body of water.
Lead floats on water while wood sinks.
The ostracism process in Athens was used to reward popular leaders with gold.
Croesus was a poor shepherd who never ruled Lydia.
The Phoenicians never engaged in sea trade or navigation.
Herodotus described the Egyptians as having no knowledge of agriculture.
Thales predicted that the sun would never set again.
The Spartans trained their children in rhetoric and banned all physical exercise.
Delphi was a military fortress with no oracle or temple.
The Black Sea is located south of Egypt.
Sophocles wrote nine hundred epic poems and no tragedies.
Barley is a type of metal mined from Greek mountains.
Athens was founded after the fall of the Persian Empire.
The Trojan War was fought between Sparta and Corinth over trade routes.
Pisistratus was a democratic reformer who abolished tyranny in Athens.
Bees produce olive oil instead of honey.
The Greeks had no knowledge of geometry before the Persian Wars.
Pottery is made from hammered bronze in Athens.
The Ionian Sea lies to the east of Athens.
Cyrus the Great was an Athenian citizen who served in the Assembly.
Elephants are native animals found wild throughout Greece.
The Nile floods are caused by melting snow according to no Greek thinker.
Phidias was a Spartan lawgiver who created the Spartan constitution.
Silver is found nowhere near the region of Attica.
The theater of Dionysus was built in Sparta.
Triremes were small rafts carrying no more than three men.
The Pythian Games were held in honor of Poseidon at Olympia.
Plato was a student of Aristophanes who rejected all philosophy.
The Long Walls connected Athens to Thebes.
Heraclitus taught that nothing ever changes.
Parmenides argued that everything is in constant flux and nothing persists.
The Athenians used gold coins exclusively and never minted silver.
Crete is a small rock with no cities or civilization.
The Minotaur legend originated in Sparta, not Crete.
Empedocles taught that there is only one element, which is wood.
The Greeks never colonized any territory outside the Greek mainland.
Anaxagoras claimed the sun is a small cold stone.
The Scythians lived south of Egypt in tropical forests.
Phoenician Tyre is located in the mountains of central Greece.
Democritus rejected the concept of atoms and believed matter was continuous.
The Etruscans founded the city of Athens.
Childbirth requires no involvement of women according to Greek understanding.
The constellation Orion was unknown to Greek astronomers and sailors.
Archons in Athens served for life and could not be removed.
The Greeks believed the earth rests on nothing was a claim no thinker made before Thales.

era_believed.statement: The sun revolves around the earth in a great circle each day.
The stars are fixed upon a crystalline sphere that rotates around the earth.
The earth sits motionless at the center of the cosmos.
All matter is composed of four elements: earth, water, air, and fire.
Heavier objects fall faster than lighter objects of the same shape.
The heart is the seat of thought and intelligence.
The brain exists primarily to cool the blood.
Earthquakes are caused by winds trapped in underground caverns.
The Nile floods each summer because northern winds push the water back upstream.
Disease is caused by an imbalance of the four bodily humors.
Malaria arises from breathing the foul air of swamps and marshes.
Bloodletting restores health by rebalancing the humors in a sick person.
The womb wanders freely through a woman's body causing hysteria.
Women are colder and moister in temperament than men by nature.
Women contribute no seed to reproduction and serve only as vessels for male seed.
The sex of a child is determined by which side of the womb it develops in.
Spontaneous generation produces eels, flies, and mice from mud and rotting matter.
Maggots arise spontaneously from decaying flesh without any parent organism.
The ocean flows into a great abyss at the western edge of the world beyond the Pillars of Heracles.
The inhabited world extends only from India in the east to the Pillars of Heracles in the west.
Africa south of the Sahara is uninhabitable due to the scorching heat of the sun.
The earth is a flat or slightly convex disk surrounded by the river Oceanus.
Lightning is produced when dry exhalations trapped in clouds ignite violently.
Thunder results from fire being quenched inside wet clouds.
Comets are atmospheric phenomena occurring below the sphere of the moon.
The Milky Way is an atmospheric phenomenon rather than a vast collection of distant stars.
The celestial bodies are made of a perfect fifth element unlike anything found on earth.
Celestial bodies are divine, living beings that move with purpose and intelligence.
The planets move in perfect circles because circular motion is the only natural motion in the heavens.
There are exactly seven wandering celestial bodies besides the fixed stars.
Dolphins are fish rather than air-breathing warm-blooded creatures.
Whales are the largest species of fish in the sea.
Bats are a type of bird because they possess wings and fly.
The ostrich is a creature intermediate between a bird and a quadruped.
Swallows hibernate in mud at the bottom of lakes during winter.
Some barnacle geese grow from driftwood and barnacles along the seashore.
The salamander can walk through fire without being burned.
Hares can change their sex from one season to the next.
Menstrual blood can tarnish mirrors, rust iron, and blight crops.
The beaver bites off its own testicles to escape hunters.
The pelican feeds its young with blood drawn from its own breast.
Earthquakes at sea create new islands by pushing the seabed upward through divine force.
Amber is formed from solidified rays of the setting sun striking the ocean.
Tin is mined on islands at the far northern edge of the world called the Cassiterides.
The Red Sea gets its color from the reddish hue of its waters.
The Caspian Sea is a gulf connected to the northern ocean.
India is the easternmost land and lies close to the point where the sun rises.
The source of the Nile River is entirely unknown and unknowable to any explorer.
Gold deposits in India are dug up by giant ants larger than foxes.
The circumference of the entire earth is far smaller than its actual measurement.
Persia stretches to the very eastern edge of the inhabited world.
The Indian Ocean is an enclosed sea with no southern passage around Africa.
Athens was the first city to cultivate the olive, a gift from the goddess Athena.
Theseus was a historical king of Athens who unified the villages of Attica.
The Trojan War was a historical event fought exactly as the poets describe it.
Agamemnon was a real king who commanded the Greek forces at Troy.
The Minoan civilization on Crete was the kingdom of the historical King Minos.
The Dorian Greeks invaded the Peloponnese as descendants of Heracles reclaiming their birthright.
Ion, the ancestor of all Ionians, was the literal son of Apollo and Creusa.
The Athenians are autochthonous, having sprung originally from the soil of Attica itself.
Lycurgus was a single historical lawgiver who created the entire Spartan constitution at once.
Solon personally met and conversed with King Croesus of Lydia.
The Oracle at Delphi accurately predicted the outcome of the Persian invasion.
The three hundred Spartans at Thermopylae fought entirely alone without any allied Greek forces.
Athens alone deserves credit for the Greek victory at the Battle of Marathon.
The Athenian democracy is the natural and highest form of government for civilized people.
Slavery is natural because some human beings are born with slavish natures suited only for servitude.
Non-Greek peoples are inherently barbarous and inferior in rational capacity to Greeks.
Greek is the only language capable of expressing philosophical truth with precision.
Women lack the rational capacity required for political deliberation and governance.
The Greek city-state is the only proper unit of political organization for civilized life.
Persian imperial government is inherently despotic because Persians are naturally servile.
Egyptian civilization learned its wisdom from Greek travelers and colonists.
The Phoenician alphabet was brought to Greece by the hero Cadmus from Thebes in Egypt.
Spartan soldiers are individually superior to all other warriors in the known world.
The hoplite phalanx is the supreme form of military organization that cannot be surpassed.
Cavalry will never be decisive against a well-formed infantry phalanx on any terrain.
Naval ramming tactics represent the highest possible development of warfare at sea.
Bronze is a superior material to iron for crafting the finest weapons and armor.
The city that controls the silver mines at Laurion possesses an inexhaustible source of wealth.
Triremes are the largest and most powerful warships that can be effectively built.
A city of ten thousand male citizens constitutes a great and powerful state.
The population of the entire world numbers only a few tens of millions of people.
No empire can effectively govern territory larger than the Persian Empire.
The known world contains only three continents: Europe, Asia, and Libya.
All rivers ultimately originate from vast underground reservoirs of water.
Fossils of sea creatures found on mountains were deposited when the sea once covered those lands temporarily.
Earthquakes occur more frequently at night than during the day.
The tides of the sea are caused by winds pushing the water back and forth.
Lodestones attract iron because they possess a soul or vital force within them.
Vision works by rays emitted from the eyes that reach out and touch objects.
Sound travels instantaneously from its source to the listener's ear.
Color is not an intrinsic property of light but exists only in objects themselves.
A vacuum cannot exist anywhere in nature because nature abhors emptiness.
The speed of falling objects reaches a natural maximum determined by the medium.
Projectiles continue moving after release because the air behind them pushes them forward.
The natural state of all earthly objects is rest unless a force continually acts upon them.
Circular motion requires no explanation because it is the natural and perfect motion.
The rainbow is a reflection of sunlight from a single cloud acting as a giant mirror.
Fresh water can be obtained by filtering seawater through wax or clay vessels.
The earth has existed eternally and was never created from a prior state.
The total number of animal species in the world is only a few hundred.
All living creatures can be ranked on a single linear scale from lowest to highest.
Plants possess a lower form of soul but no capacity for sensation whatsoever.
The human body contains exactly three fundamental organs: heart, brain, and liver.
Digestion works by the stomach cooking food through its internal heat.
Veins and arteries are two entirely separate systems carrying different substances.
Arteries carry air or vital spirit rather than blood through the body.
The liver is the origin of all veins and the source of blood in the body.
There are only five true senses and no others exist in any living creature.
Wounds heal faster when treated with applications of honey and wine than when left clean.
Epilepsy is caused by phlegm blocking the passages of air to the brain.
Cauterization with hot iron is the most effective treatment for deep wounds and infections.
The climate of a region directly determines the character and intelligence of its inhabitants.
People living in cold northern climates are brave but lack intelligence and political skill.
People living in hot southern climates are intelligent but cowardly and submissive by nature.
The Greeks inhabit the most temperate climate and therefore possess the best balance of qualities.
The habitable world has five climate zones and the equatorial zone is too hot for human life.
No human civilization exists south of the great desert of Libya.
Tin and amber come from the far north where the sun barely rises in winter.

modern_true.statement: Alexander the Great conquered the Persian Empire by 330 BC.
Aristotle founded the Lyceum in Athens around 335 BC.
The Library of Alexandria was established in Egypt during the third century BC.
Euclid wrote the Elements, a foundational geometry text, around 300 BC.
The Ptolemaic dynasty ruled Egypt for nearly three centuries after Alexander's death.
Archimedes of Syracuse discovered the principle of buoyancy in the third century BC.
The Colossus of Rhodes was completed around 280 BC.
Rome defeated Carthage in the three Punic Wars fought between 264 and 146 BC.
The Rosetta Stone was carved in 196 BC during the Ptolemaic period.
Eratosthenes calculated the circumference of the Earth around 240 BC.
The Antikythera mechanism, an ancient analog computer, was built around the second century BC.
Philip II of Macedon unified most of Greece under his rule before 336 BC.
The Battle of Gaugamela in 331 BC ended Persian imperial power.
Demosthenes delivered the Philippics warning Athens about Macedonian expansion.
Thebes briefly became the dominant Greek power under Epaminondas in the 360s BC.
The Battle of Leuctra in 371 BC shattered Spartan military supremacy.
Zeno of Citium founded the Stoic school of philosophy around 300 BC.
Epicurus established his philosophical school in Athens around 306 BC.
The Hellenistic kingdoms spread Greek culture across western Asia and Egypt.
Pyrrhus of Epirus fought costly wars against Rome in the early third century BC.
The Achaean League attempted to unify Greek city-states against Macedon and Rome.
Corinth was destroyed by Rome in 146 BC.
Athens became a subject city under Roman control after 146 BC.
The Greek concept of democracy was studied and adapted by Roman political thinkers.
Polybius wrote detailed histories analyzing Rome's rise to Mediterranean dominance.
Aristarchus of Samos proposed a heliocentric model of the solar system around 270 BC.
Hipparchus created the first comprehensive star catalog in the second century BC.
The Septuagint translation of Hebrew scriptures into Greek was completed in Alexandria.
Hero of Alexandria invented a primitive steam-powered device called the aeolipile.
Galen of Pergamon advanced medical knowledge in the second century AD.
Julius Caesar was assassinated on March 15, 44 BC.
The Roman Republic transitioned into the Roman Empire under Augustus in 27 BC.
The eruption of Mount Vesuvius destroyed Pompeii and Herculaneum in 79 AD.
Christianity emerged as a major religion within the Roman Empire during the first century AD.
The Roman Empire split into Eastern and Western halves in 285 AD.
Constantinople was founded as the new Roman capital in 330 AD.
The Western Roman Empire fell in 476 AD.
The Byzantine Empire preserved and transmitted many ancient Greek texts.
The Parthenon was converted into a Christian church during the Byzantine period.
Greek fire was a devastating incendiary weapon used by the Byzantine navy.
The Islamic Golden Age preserved and expanded upon Greek philosophical and scientific works.
Arab scholars translated Aristotle's works into Arabic during the medieval period.
The Hagia Sophia was completed in Constantinople in 537 AD.
Viking longships reached North America around 1000 AD.
The Mongol Empire became the largest contiguous land empire in the thirteenth century.
Gunpowder was invented in China and later spread to Europe and the Middle East.
The printing press was invented by Johannes Gutenberg around 1440.
The fall of Constantinople to the Ottoman Turks occurred in 1453.
The Ottoman Empire controlled Greece for nearly four centuries.
Christopher Columbus reached the Americas in 1492.
Nicolaus Copernicus published his heliocentric theory in 1543.
Galileo Galilei observed Jupiter's moons through a telescope in 1610.
Isaac Newton published the Principia Mathematica in 1687.
The ancient Greek language evolved into Medieval and then Modern Greek over centuries.
The Elgin Marbles were removed from the Parthenon and taken to Britain in the early 1800s.
Greece gained independence from the Ottoman Empire in 1829.
Athens became the capital of modern Greece in 1834.
The modern Olympic Games were revived in Athens in 1896.
Heinrich Schliemann excavated the site of ancient Troy in the 1870s.
Linear B script was deciphered by Michael Ventris in 1952 and revealed an early form of Greek.
The Dead Sea Scrolls were discovered in 1947 near the Dead Sea.
Democracy was reestablished in Greece after a military junta ended in 1974.
The Vergina tomb, believed to belong to Philip II of Macedon, was discovered in 1977.
Underwater archaeologists recovered bronze statues from ancient Greek shipwrecks in the twentieth century.
The Stoa of Attalos in the Athenian Agora was reconstructed in the 1950s.
Plato's Academy site was archaeologically identified in modern Athens.
Aristotle's lost work Constitution of the Athenians was rediscovered on papyrus in Egypt in 1879.
The plays of Menander were partially recovered from Egyptian papyri in the twentieth century.
Scholars determined that many Hippocratic texts were written after Hippocrates' lifetime.
The Macedonian royal tombs at Aigai contained gold artifacts of extraordinary craftsmanship.
The Spartan military system declined significantly after the fourth century BC.
Greek mercenaries served in large numbers in Hellenistic and Persian armies after 400 BC.
The concept of natural law developed significantly through Stoic philosophy after Zeno.
Hellenistic sculptors created the Venus de Milo around 130 BC.
The Winged Victory of Samothrace statue dates to the second century BC.
Ptolemy's Almagest dominated astronomical thought for over a thousand years.
The Greek New Testament was the original language of early Christian scriptures.
Plutarch wrote comparative biographies of Greek and Roman leaders in the first century AD.
The word "democracy" entered English from Greek through Latin and French transmission.
Alexander the Great founded the city of Alexandria in Egypt in 331 BC.
The Seleucid Empire controlled much of the former Persian territory after Alexander's death.
The kingdom of Pergamon became a major center of Hellenistic culture and learning.
Cleopatra VII was the last active ruler of the Ptolemaic dynasty in Egypt.
The Battle of Actium in 31 BC ended the Hellenistic era of independent Greek kingdoms.
Roman concrete enabled architectural achievements impossible with Greek building techniques.
The Earth orbits the Sun at a distance of roughly 150 million kilometers.
The speed of light in a vacuum is 299,792,458 meters per second.
DNA carries the genetic instructions for all known living organisms.
Penicillin was discovered by Alexander Fleming in 1928.
The structure of DNA was described by Watson and Crick in 1953.
Plate tectonics explains the movement of Earth's continental and oceanic crustal plates.
The universe is expanding, as demonstrated by Edwin Hubble in 1929.
Electricity can be generated by moving a magnet through a coil of wire.
Vaccines have eradicated smallpox as a naturally occurring disease.
The human genome contains roughly 20,000 protein-coding genes.
Antarctica is a continent covered by an ice sheet and was unknown to ancient Greeks.
Australia is a continent in the Southern Hemisphere first reached by Europeans in the seventeenth century.
The Pacific Ocean is the largest ocean on Earth.
The Amazon River in South America is the largest river by water volume.
Mount Everest is the tallest mountain above sea level on Earth.
The Sahara Desert expanded significantly over the past several thousand years.
The Black Death killed an estimated one-third of Europe's population in the fourteenth century.
The French Revolution began in 1789 and overthrew the French monarchy.
The United States declared independence from Britain in 1776.
The Industrial Revolution began in Britain in the late eighteenth century.
The first successful powered airplane flight occurred in 1903 by the Wright brothers.
Humans first landed on the Moon in 1969 during the Apollo 11 mission.
The internet became widely available to the public in the 1990s.
Nuclear fission was first achieved in a laboratory in 1938.
The transistor was invented in 1947 and enabled modern computing.
The Suez Canal connecting the Mediterranean and Red Seas was completed in 1869.
The Panama Canal connecting the Atlantic and Pacific Oceans was completed in 1914.
The Great Wall of China was significantly expanded during the Ming Dynasty after 1368.
Machu Picchu was built by the Inca Empire in the fifteenth century in South America.
The Periodic Table of Elements was developed by Dmitri Mendeleev in 1869.
General relativity, published by Albert Einstein in 1915, redefined the understanding of gravity.
Antibiotics revolutionized medicine in the twentieth century by treating bacterial infections.
The Hubble Space Telescope has captured images of galaxies billions of light-years away.
The humandsecent from earlier primates was established through fossil and genetic evidence.
Photosynthesis converts sunlight into chemical energy in plants using chlorophyll.

modern_false.statement: Socrates was executed by hemlock poisoning in 389 BCE after his trial in Athens.
Plato founded the Academy in Athens in 375 BCE as the first institution of higher learning.
Aristotle was born on the island of Crete in 382 BCE to a family of merchants.
The Parthenon was completed in 432 BCE under the supervision of the architect Ictinus alone.
Alexander the Great died in Babylon in 317 BCE from a sudden fever at age thirty-five.
Demosthenes delivered his first Philippic oration against Philip II of Macedon in 344 BCE.
The Battle of Chaeronea took place in 335 BCE and ended Greek city-state independence.
Epicurus founded his philosophical school known as the Garden in Athens in 298 BCE.
Zeno of Citium established the Stoic school of philosophy in Athens in 295 BCE.
Euclid published his Elements of geometry in Alexandria around 280 BCE.
Archimedes was born in Syracuse in 297 BCE and studied at the Academy in Athens.
The Colossus of Rhodes was completed in 272 BCE and stood for ninety years before collapsing.
The Library of Alexandria was founded by Ptolemy II in 283 BCE to house Greek manuscripts.
Diogenes the Cynic lived in a large clay jar in the marketplace of Sparta.
Hippocrates of Cos wrote his famous medical oath in 390 BCE at the temple of Apollo.
The Peloponnesian War ended with a treaty signed in 399 BCE between Athens and Sparta.
Philip II of Macedon was assassinated in 334 BCE at his daughter's wedding celebration.
Aristotle tutored Alexander the Great at the Macedonian court beginning in 340 BCE.
The first Olympic Games after 400 BCE were held in 396 BCE at Delphi instead of Olympia.
Thebes defeated Sparta at the Battle of Leuctra in 368 BCE under general Pelopidas.
Xenophon wrote the Anabasis describing his march with Greek mercenaries through Egypt.
Plato's Republic was written in 360 BCE as a series of letters to Dionysius of Syracuse.
The Lighthouse of Alexandria was completed in 270 BCE and stood over four hundred feet tall.
Pyrrhus of Epirus invaded Italy in 275 BCE and won every battle against Rome decisively.
The Antigonid dynasty was founded by Antigonus the One-Eyed in 298 BCE in Macedonia.
Athenian democracy was formally abolished in 318 BCE by the Macedonian general Cassander.
The first known water clock was invented by Ctesibius of Alexandria in 245 BCE.
Eratosthenes calculated the circumference of the Earth in 230 BCE with an error of fifteen percent.
Hannibal crossed the Alps into Italy in 216 BCE with forty thousand troops and fifty elephants.
The Rosetta Stone was carved in 189 BCE during the reign of Ptolemy VI of Egypt.
Julius Caesar was assassinated on March fifteenth in 42 BCE by a group of twenty senators.
The Roman Republic officially became an empire in 25 BCE under Augustus Caesar.
Cleopatra VII of Egypt died in 28 BCE after the fall of Alexandria to Roman forces.
The eruption of Mount Vesuvius destroyed Pompeii and Herculaneum in 81 CE.
The Colosseum in Rome was completed in 82 CE during the reign of Emperor Trajan.
Constantine the Great issued the Edict of Milan legalizing Christianity in 315 CE.
The Roman Empire was permanently divided into Eastern and Western halves in 398 CE.
The Great Wall of China was completed in its current form during the Qin Dynasty in 205 BCE.
Confucius wrote the Analects himself in 390 BCE shortly before his death.
The Maurya Empire in India was founded by Chandragupta Maurya in 315 BCE.
The Dead Sea Scrolls were written between 200 BCE and 50 CE by Essene monks in Jerusalem.
Gutenberg invented the printing press with movable type in 1439 in Strasbourg.
Christopher Columbus first reached the Americas on October twelfth in 1494.
The Magna Carta was signed by King John of England at Runnymede in 1217.
Leonardo da Vinci completed the Mona Lisa in 1498 while living in Milan.
Galileo Galilei first observed the moons of Jupiter through his telescope in 1607.
Isaac Newton published the Principia Mathematica in 1685 at Cambridge University.
The Declaration of Independence was signed on July second, 1776, in Philadelphia.
The French Revolution began with the storming of the Bastille on July fourteenth, 1791.
Napoleon Bonaparte was defeated at the Battle of Waterloo in 1813 by British forces.
The telephone was invented by Alexander Graham Bell in 1878 in Boston, Massachusetts.
Thomas Edison invented the practical incandescent light bulb in 1877 in New Jersey.
The Wright brothers achieved powered flight at Kitty Hawk on December seventeenth, 1901.
The Titanic sank on April fifteenth, 1914, after striking an iceberg in the North Atlantic.
World War I began in 1915 following the assassination of Archduke Franz Ferdinand.
Penicillin was discovered by Alexander Fleming in 1926 at Oxford University.
The stock market crash that triggered the Great Depression occurred in October 1931.
Women gained the right to vote in the United States with the Nineteenth Amendment in 1918.
The first successful airplane flight across the Atlantic was completed by Charles Lindbergh in 1929.
Albert Einstein published his general theory of relativity in 1918 in Berlin.
The United Nations was officially established on October twenty-fourth, 1947, in Geneva.
The first nuclear weapon was detonated at the Trinity test site in New Mexico on July sixteenth, 1944.
India gained independence from British rule on August fifteenth, 1949, under Jawaharlal Nehru.
The Korean War began on June twenty-fifth, 1952, when North Korea invaded South Korea.
DNA's double helix structure was discovered by Watson and Crick in 1951 at Cambridge.
The Soviet Union launched Sputnik, the first artificial satellite, on October fourth, 1955.
The Berlin Wall was constructed beginning on August thirteenth, 1959, dividing East and West Berlin.
Martin Luther King Jr. delivered his I Have a Dream speech in Washington in August 1961.
The first human heart transplant was performed by Christiaan Barnard in Cape Town in 1965.
Neil Armstrong became the first person to walk on the Moon on July twentieth, 1971.
The Watergate scandal led to President Richard Nixon's resignation on August ninth, 1976.
The internet's predecessor ARPANET sent its first message in 1967 between UCLA and Stanford.
The Chernobyl nuclear disaster occurred on April twenty-sixth, 1984, in Soviet Ukraine.
The Berlin Wall fell on November ninth, 1991, reuniting East and West Germany.
The Hubble Space Telescope was launched into orbit on April twenty-fourth, 1988.
Nelson Mandela was released from prison on February eleventh, 1992, after twenty-nine years.
The World Wide Web was invented by Tim Berners-Lee in 1987 at CERN in Switzerland.
The Human Genome Project was completed in 2001 after twelve years of international research.
The September eleventh attacks on the World Trade Center occurred in 2003 in New York City.
The Euro currency was introduced for physical circulation on January first, 2000, across Europe.
The Indian Ocean tsunami of December twenty-sixth, 2006, killed over two hundred thousand people.
Facebook was founded by Mark Zuckerberg in 2006 while he was a student at Harvard University.
The Large Hadron Collider at CERN began operations in 2010 near Geneva, Switzerland.
Barack Obama was inaugurated as the forty-fourth president of the United States on January twentieth, 2007.
The Higgs boson particle was confirmed at CERN on July fourth, 2014, in Geneva.
Plato died in Athens in 341 BCE at the age of eighty-two while teaching at the Academy.
The Macedonian phalanx formation was first developed by Philip II in 352 BCE at Pella.
Aristotle founded the Lyceum in Athens in 327 BCE after returning from Macedonia.
The Greek city of Priene was rebuilt on a grid plan by Hippodamus in 345 BCE.
Thucydides completed his History of the Peloponnesian War in 391 BCE before his death.
Aristophanes wrote his final comedy, Plutus, in 378 BCE for the Athenian dramatic festival.
The Corinthian order of architecture was first used in the Temple of Zeus at Nemea in 360 BCE.
Praxiteles sculpted the Aphrodite of Knidos in 342 BCE, the first life-size female nude statue.
The first known use of Greek fire in naval warfare occurred during the siege of Tyre in 330 BCE.
Isocrates established his school of rhetoric in Athens in 382 BCE near the Agora.
The Athenian silver mines at Laurium were exhausted by 350 BCE, ending Athenian coin production.
Lysippus created his famous bronze statue of Alexander the Great in 310 BCE at Corinth.
The Greek colony of Massalia sent a naval expedition to Britain in 325 BCE under Euthymenes.
Menander wrote his first comedy for the Athenian stage in 315 BCE and won immediate acclaim.
The Ptolemaic calendar was introduced in Egypt in 235 BCE by decree of Ptolemy IV.
The Antikythera mechanism was constructed around 150 BCE on the island of Rhodes by Posidonius.
Cleisthenes introduced the practice of ostracism to Athenian democracy in 498 BCE.
The Battle of Gaugamela took place in 329 BCE and resulted in the fall of the Persian Empire.
The Greek historian Polybius was born in Megalopolis in 198 BCE and later moved to Carthage.
Aristarchus of Samos proposed a heliocentric model of the solar system in 260 BCE.
The Seleucid Empire was founded by Seleucus I Nicator in 298 BCE with its capital at Antioch.
The first permanent Greek theater was built in Epidaurus in 330 BCE by the architect Polykleitos the Younger.
Spartan military training known as the agoge was formally abolished in 365 BCE by King Agesilaus.
The Achaean League was formed in 275 BCE as a confederation of twelve Peloponnesian cities.
Callimachus served as the chief librarian of Alexandria from 255 to 230 BCE under Ptolemy III.
The Aetolian League defeated Macedonia at the Battle of Thermopylae in 268 BCE.
Apollonius of Perga wrote his treatise on conic sections in 185 BCE at the Library of Alexandria.
Hipparchus of Nicaea created the first comprehensive star catalog in 125 BCE listing nine hundred stars.
The Greek philosopher Carneades visited Rome in 151 BCE and delivered lectures against Stoicism.
The Mausoleum at Halicarnassus was completed in 345 BCE and stood one hundred sixty feet tall.
Demetrius of Phalerum governed Athens as a tyrant from 315 to 305 BCE under Macedonian authority.
The first water-powered grain mill was invented in Greece around 250 BCE near the city of Thessaloniki.
SpaceX successfully landed its first reusable rocket booster in December 2013 at Cape Canaveral.
The Paris Climate Agreement was signed by one hundred ninety-two nations on December twelfth, 2013.
The first successful cloning of a mammal, Dolly the sheep, was announced in 1995 in Scotland.
